layer.label: Sinai Arabic NF M 66, Undertext (Greek majuscule)

note: Very small Alexandrian majuscule with influences of Biblical majuscule (in the letters mu, pi, omega). Critical signs, diacritics, and interlinear corrections were added by a later hand (7th-8th c. CE?)

This text comes from a luxurious manuscript, whose folios measured approximately 300 x 250 mm (Turner group II). The letters are 3 mm high and 2-4 mm wide, the space between the lines is 7-8 mm. The margins are large: the internal margin measures 30 mm, the external margin 50 mm, the upper margin 40 mm (the lower margin is not preserved). The written surface is 175 x 155 mm. At the end of the lines the letters are smaller, in order to respect the line division and the justification of the margins, which is a feature typical of luxury codices

note.value: Previously unknown hexameters of mythological content on the childhood of Dionysus. The presence of proparoxytone words at the end of the verses, of spondaic verses, and of consecutive spondees rules out the attribution of this work to a follower of the Nonnus of Panopolis school and of his "modern style." The style is rather archaic, as in the uninterrupted reuse of Homeric formulae at the beginning and end of the verses
Description by Giulia Rossetto
Bibliography: Rossetto 2021 ; Rossetto, et al. ; Rossetto 2023b
Reconstructed folio order, ff. 2r+7r+8v, 2v+7v+8r; 6r, 6v
The fragmentary fols. 2r+7r+8v as well as 2v+7v+8r were originally part of one original folio
Description of undertext by Giulia Rossetto
Bibliography: Rossetto 2021 ; Rossetto, et al. ; Rossetto 2023b

work.desc_title: Martyrdom of Partiklos of Caesarea, Follower of Pamphilos

note.value: Earliest attestation of this text in CPA. It is only known from a list as Baṭrīqlāwus (Arabic) and Patrikila (Georgian) in Sinai Georgian 34; see Sauget, pp. 316-317
Description by Christa Müller-Kessler

Bibliography: J.-M. Sauget, Premières recherches sur l’origine et les charactéristiques des synaxaires melkites (XIe-XVIIe siècles), Subsidia Hagiographica 45 (Brussels, 1969), pp. 316-317 ; Desreumaux 2010, p. 1322, fig. 2 (photo of f. 5v) ; Müller-Kessler 2018a, pp. 63-71
Reconstructed folio order, ff. 5(I)v, 5(I)r, 5(II)v, 5(II)r, 1(I)r, 1(I)v, 1(II)r, 1(II)v
Difficult to identify the sequence, since the contents has been unknown. One Arabic folio consists of two original CPA folios. Roman numerals refer to the original CPA folios
Description of undertext by Christa Müller-Kessler

Bibliography: J.-M. Sauget, Premières recherches sur l’origine et les charactéristiques des synaxaires melkites (XIe-XVIIe siècles), Subsidia Hagiographica 45 (Brussels, 1969), pp. 316-317 ; Desreumaux 2010, p. 1322, fig. 2 (photo of f. 5v) ; Müller-Kessler 2018a, pp. 63-71

layer.label: Sinai Arabic NF M 66, Undertext (Greek majuscule)

note: Only a few letters are visible. The parchment fragment is too small to reconstruct the number of columns and lines, but it is clear that the folio was made up from stitching together multiple pieces of parchment

note.value: Description by Giulia Rossetto

Biliography: H. L. Fleischer, "Über einen griechisch-arabischen Codex rescriptus der Leipziger Universitäts-Bibliothek," in Zeitschrift der deutschen morgenländischen Gesellschaft 1 (1847), p. 148-160 (repr. in ibid., Kleinere Schriften III [Leipzig, 1888], p. 379-388, n. xxii), with a plate ; H. L. Fleischer, “Beschreibung der von Prof. Dr. Tischendorf im J. 1853 aus dem Morgenlande zurückgebrachten christlich-arabischen Handschriften,” in Zeitschrift der deutschen morgenländischen Gesellschaft 8 (1854), p. 584-587 ; N. Tchernetska, “Cambridge University Library Add. 1879.5 and Its Provenance," in Homer, Tragedy and Beyond: Essays in Honour of P. E. Easterling, ed. F. Budelmann, P. Michelakis (London, 2001), p. 201-208 ; M. van Esbroeck, “Le codex rescriptus Tischendorf 2 à Leipzig et Cyrille de Scythopolis en version arabe,” in Actes du deuxième Congrès international d’études arabes chrétiennes (Oosterhesselen, September 1984), ed. S. K. Samir (OCA 226), Rome, 1986, p. 81-91 ; G. Rossetto, "Fragments from the Orphic Rhapsodies? Hitherto Unknown Hexameters in the Palimpsest Sin. ar. NF 66," Zeitschrift für Papyrologie und Epigraphik, 219, 2021, 34–60 ; Rossetto 2021, pp. 34-60

Additional parts of this manuscript can be found in three further manuscripts: Leipzig, Univ. gr. 2 (22 folios) = Codex Tischendorf 2; St. Petersburg, RNB, gr. 26 (6 folios); Cambridge Add. 1879.5 (one small fragment). The characteristics of the layout and of the script suggest that this text originally belonged to an Old Testament manuscript (with additional parts in Leipzig, Cambridge, St. Petersburg) or to a grammatical text (with additional parts in Leipzig only)
Description of undertext by Giulia Rossetto
